prompt: An aerial night view from high above, looking down at a dark landscape shaped loosely like northeastern Africa. Across this darkness are thousands and thousands of tiny points of light — small glowing embers and hearth fires, each one representing a home, a family. But the vast, overwhelming majority of these lights have been extinguished. Cold gray ash marks where fires once burned. Faint wisps of smoke rise from thousands of extinguished points. Only a small fraction — perhaps one in ten — still glows with warm amber light. The darkness spreading across the image is immense and suffocating. The few remaining lights appear fragile, isolated, small against the overwhelming dark. The visual language is: light equals home, darkness equals displacement and loss. No maps, no borders, no country outlines drawn explicitly — just the organic shape of light and darkness. No text, no labels, no legends, no numbers, no charts. Deeply mournful atmosphere. Color palette: deep indigo and black for darkness, warm amber and gold for surviving fires, cold gray-white for extinguished ash. Fine art photography or painterly illustration. Extremely high detail on the individual light points.
insight: Sudan's war has led to one of the worst humanitarian crisis. ACLED recorded over 28,700 fatalities by late 2024 and the war has also led to the displacement of upto 14 million people.
